column1: 42;"Kiertotalouden ekosysteemit ja ekoinnovaatiot The thesis examines the types of circular economy ecosystems and eco-innovations that have been established
column2: as well as the projects that have been developed or are being developed around them in the coming years. The thesis also examines the role of digitalization in circular economy projects and the importance of artificial intelligence in innovating
column3: establishing ecosystems and analysing data challenges in cooperation with other actors. The study was commissioned by the DAVE- project whose goal is to establish data-based innovation environments that would enable organizations to change their own operations in a more sustainable and environmentally friendly direction by utilizing digitalization. The project develops the circular economy in cooperation with other actors by exchanging data and information.

column1: Dozens of circular economy ecosystems were found therefore the research was carried out using different data management methods and comparative research. Artificial intelligence also played a significant role in the acquisition and structuring of material. One form of the research was to utilize artificial intelligence in the identification of eco-innovations. The research made extensive use of material found on the Internet and scientific publications.
The results of the research revealed that numerous circular economy ecosystems and eco-innovations have been developed
column2: and more projects and innovations are continuously being developed around these themes. There is a wide demand for the subject even outside Finland
column3: and the work is done in a multidisciplinary manner between countries widely across Europe. The work is done in cooperation between different operators and the aim is to reach a result where each operator would benefit from the projects. The research results are also valuable for the DAVE- project

column1: 51;"Development of Time Management and Leadership KPIs for Managers: A Literature Review-Based Approach This thesis bridges a critical performance management gap by developing coherent managerial performance Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) that measure effectiveness in both time management and leadership domains. Virtually all contemporary KPI systems adopt a classical approach that compart-mentalizes leadership and time management as mutually exclusive fields. This produces disjointed and often confusing indicators of managerial capability
column2: despite organizations increasingly demanding quantifiable measures in performance reviews. In response to the unsustainable effects of existing KPI systemsincluding burnout and short-sighted attentionthis thesis incorporates the tenets of sustainable development as its core. The resulting KPI framework tracks and rewards managerial actions that ensure both organizational longevity and reform
column3: as well as societal improvement
column4: aligning business success with employee well-being and a positive impact on society. This study identifies
column5: evaluates
column6: and analyzes prevalent KPIs through a thorough literature review carried out in compliance with PRISMA guidelines. This reveals significant shortcomings
column7: including quantitative bias
column8: behavioral distortion (also known as Goodhart's Law)
column9: strategic misalignment

column1: 71;"Toipumisorientaatio mielenterveys- ja pðihdekuntoutujien asumispalveluissa - ohje omaohjaajalle The purpose of this functional thesis was to create written guidelines for the job description of a personal counsellor working in a housing unit operated by Kotia Palvelut Oy
column2: which offers housing services for persons undergoing mental health and substance abuse rehabilitation. Kotia Palvelut Oy is located in Oulu and Nokia. The purpose was to make a set of clear and comprehensive guidelines that described the work of a personal counsellor to be included in the company's orientation material. The aim of the thesis was to develop the personal counselling expertise of the staff within the framework of recovery orientation.

column1: The housing unit for mental health and substance rehabilitees supports the rehabilitees in
dividually and as well as possible in shaping their daily lives and provides assistance in var
ious situations. They do rehabilitative work with their clients. Mental health and substance
abuse work has moved from a professional-centered way of working to a client-oriented
one. Therefore
column2: the guidelines for the job description of a personal counsellor focus specifically on client involvement. The theoretical framework of this thesis is based on the concept of recovery orientation and emphasizes a motivating
column3: dialogic and solution oriented approach in personal counselling. The conclusion is that personal counsellors play a major role in and are responsible for the daily lives and activities of mental health and substance abuse rehabilitees. A personal counsellor supports the client in different situations
column4: providing them with his/her professional expertise. The guidelines facilitate the orientation of new employees and students. They are not just a to-do list; they discuss the different aspects of personal counsellors work. The guidelines support the work of the personal counsellor and serve as a tool for them. The commissioner of this thesis regarded the product as good as it considers the client's individuality
column5: inclusion and rehabilitation process in a holistic way. The building of a trust relationship between the personal counsellor and the client and genuine presence were also considered important features of the product.";0;NO_INDICATION_OF_COLLABORATION;Toipumisorientaatio mielenterveys- ja pðihdekuntoutujien asumispalveluissa - ohje omaohjaajalle;Kajaani UAS;"Hemmilð

column1: 72;"Alueellinen vaikuttavuus TKI-toiminnassa: Kajaanin AMK Kainuun kehittðjðnð This thesis examined the role of Kajaani University of Applied Sciences (KAMK) in the research

column1: The research used a mixed-methods approach combining quantitative and qualitative data. The quantitative part is based on publicly available statistics
column2: which were collected and analysed to form a long-term view of RDI activity in the region. The qualitative material was garnered through semi-structured interviews with four companies that had collaborated with KAMK in RDI projects. The interview data were analysed using content analysis.

column2: and although its total RDI investments remained moderate

column6: being more proactive in approaching companies and strengthening knowledge specific to a sector

column1: 74;Key Success Factors in the  Finnish Video Game Industry: Rovio and Supercell The topic of this thesis was to present key success factors from the Finnish video game industry by focusing on Rovio and Supercell to assess if their methods for success are still relevant in the industry during the 2020s. The thesis presents industry related concepts from the global and Finnish video game industry in the 2020s before delving into Rovio and Supercell. column1: 81;Applying Lean Principles in the HORECA Industry: Enhancing Value Creation and Minimizing Waste for Sustainable Growth. This study analyzes lean principle adoption within Finnish HORECA (Hotels

column3: and Cafes) sector through examination of Restel Oy which operates more than 200 outlets. The research investigates the impact of lean methodologies on operation waste reduction and sustainability together with performance enhancement in service-oriented settings. Both qualitative management insights about lean implementation and cultural practices were gathered and quantitative data covered employee training requirements and workflow efficiency and customer survey results. The research demonstrates that Restel Oy decreased food waste successfully yet lean integration produced different results based on its restaurant types. column1: 115;"Strategic software product management : designing a template for product strategy and product roadmap Startegy is a complex topic and many companies fail to define a strategy or fail to adapt the strategy to changing conditions around them. In software development product strategy is crucial to understand who the product is for and what value it brings. In addition the connection between company strategy and product strategy is important as both influence each other and misalignment can be a costly mistake.

The purpose of the thesis was to explore how to create a product strategy and a roadmap for new software products from the perspective of the product management role. The aim is to answer questions: what is a strategy? How to build a product vision and product strategy in the context of new software development from the perspective of product management? How are business strategy and product strategy complementary? And how the defined strategy turns into execution in the form of a roadmap?

The outcome of the research was a new strategy canvas template that combines the theoretical research into one visualization that can help product teams craft a vision and product strategy for new products. The method of the thesis was research based development work and the goal was to distill the theory into a practical framework. The inspiration for the thesis comes from the writers work experience as a product manager.";0;NO_INDICATION_OF_COLLABORATION;Strategic software product management : designing a template for product strategy and product roadmap;Laurea UAS;Aho

column1: The purpose of this research-based developmen work was to develop higher educational offering in the field of space business related studies and to understand the value formation of potential students regarding space business education. The development work examined what kind of needs potential students have in the field and what kind of multidiciplinary course content is required.

The theoretical framework of this thesis is based on the Finnish space industry and the national space strategy designed to guide the sectors development. This strategy also guides higher education institutions in creating programs that support the growth of the space sector. Within the higher education context
column2: the development work examines the Finnish higher education system
column3: and relevant policies. Work life competence is also analyzed from the perspectives of skills
column4: expertise
column5: and dynamic capabilities. The study presents a service ecosystem that illustrates how value is created for the customer through different business logics.

column1: The methods used for the development work included future foresight in the preliminary phase and service design in the development phase. The Stanford Design Schools hexagon model was used in the service design process. The methods of data collection were benchmarking research
column2: observation
column3: survey and interviews. The survey responses of users interested in space business studies were compared with the results of the content analysis of the interviews. With this gathered information three customer personas and customer journeys were developed and used to identify relevant course content as well as the needs and motivation of the potential customers of the planned services.

column1: As a result
column2: a concept was developed for Laurea which included suggestions for multidiciplinary studies that Laurea could provide in the field of the space industry. The model starts with individual courses and later on expands to study modules that can be integrated into degree programs. The findings of this development work emphasize the need for studies that integrate theory with practice and maintain strong connections to working life. Students see significant value in such programs
column3: as they require new skills to differentiate themselves as job applicants.

column1: For further development

column5: cost

column1: 157;"Enhancing Sleeping Cell Detection Through Comparing Two Internal Mobile Network Analysing Tools Sleeping cells represent a challenging issue in mobile networks

column5: where available logs and performance counters have proven insufficient to reliably investigate such failures.

column1: The thesis proposes and implements a method that enables the detection and investigation of sleeping cells using available measurement data and test  procedures. The method is first validated through manual testing to assess its effectiveness
column2: after which a framework for automating the approach is designed and implemented. The study also examines throughput-related aspects and provides a comparison of two internal tools for network analysis. The thesis also includes a survey aimed at evaluating the tool and its automation.

column4: the work offers immediate value in the test environment and lays a foundation for the future automation in our environment.";0;NO_INDICATION_OF_COLLABORATION;Enhancing Sleeping Cell Detection Through Comparing Two Internal Mobile Network Analysing Tools;Oulu UAS;Passoja

column1: 165;"Improving Exception Handling in L1 The thesis studied exception handling and explored its possible improvements. The goal was to improve exception handling in L1 level application by improving error logging and making the code more robust. 5G architecture
column2: error and exception handling were studied to understand how they work.

column1: 5G architecture consist of three main components: Radio Access Network
column2: Core Network and Transport Network. 5GC makes it possible to establish connection in the network
column3: RAN connects User Equipment to the core network and Transport Network makes data transfer possible between RAN and 5GC. Layer 1 handles data transfer and reception over the air interface.

column1: Error detection is a crucial part of a programs functionality
column2: and some common issues are invalid input
column3: null pointers and memory problems. Errors can be handled with logging
column4: exceptions
column5: input validation and error codes. Exception handling is a great tool for error handling
column6: but it comes with its disadvantages. Exceptions can hold much information about the failure and be easily propagated but on the other hand can lead to resource leaks or in extensive use affect the codes performance.

column1: A few cases were studied to understand how exceptions were handled in the code. One handler function that could handle exceptions in every layer of the code was studied to be used in a wider scope and two implementations were analysed. One focused more on propagating exceptions and providing logs layer by layer while the other one was more focused on a simple and readable handler. The exception propagation implementation was more complex and heavier code but could provide more detailed information. The handler function implementation was simple and readable but could only be used in its own specified location.

Both presented implementations proved to have their own advantages and limitations and therefore it was concluded it would either depend on the situation or it should be decided internally which would be a better fit. The thesis offered possible study points for future projects
column2: for example if the implementations could be combined
column3: a more detailed study about them and how exception handling could be done in each layer.";0;NO_INDICATION_OF_COLLABORATION;Improving Exception Handling in L1;Oulu UAS;Uusitalo

column1: 170;"Sustainability Reporting as a Signal  to Job Seekers This thesis examines sustainability reporting as a signal to job seekers and explores how such reporting influences their employment decisions in the Oulu region. This topic remains underexplored
column2: particularly regarding how job seekers interpret sustainability reports when evaluating potential employers.

column1: The study applies the Triple Bottom Line (TBL) framework. The study investigates which of the sustainability dimensions
column2: environmental
column3: social
column4: or economic
column5: is prioritized by job seekers
column6: what challenges they face when interpreting sustainability reports
column7: and whether job seekers believe the importance of sustainability changes once employed. To complement these perspectives
column8: job advertisements from a major technology company in the region were analysed to identify how TBL dimensions appear in job advertisements.

column1: The theoretical framework integrates the TBL model with Signaling Theory and considers the role of established reporting frameworks
column2: including the Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) and the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD)
column3: in shaping corporate sustainability reporting.

column1: A mixed-methods design was applied. The core of the empirical part was the quantitative and qualitative survey conducted among potential job seekers in the Oulu region (n=10). Additional insights were gained by deductive qualitative analysis of job advertisements from an example company (n=14)
column2: which were analysed using TBL-related keywords.

column1: The findings of this thesis relate to the research questions. Sustainability reporting has a moderate influence on job seekers decision-making
column2: but the importance increases if sustainability reporting is clearer since job seekers face challenges in interpreting the reports. Job seekers prioritize the social dimension over environmental and economic dimensions. Furthermore
column3: job seekers perceive that sustainability becomes more important once employed.

column1: This thesis concludes that sustainability reporting can support job seekers in decision-making
column2: but its influence depends on the clarity and accessibility of the information provided. The results suggest that companies may enhance employer attractiveness by presenting sustainability information in a more concise
column3: clear
column4: and transparent format.";0;NO_INDICATION_OF_COLLABORATION;Sustainability Reporting as a Signal  to Job Seekers;Satakunnan UAS;Hðmðlðinen

column1: 177;"UN Sustainability Development Goals in Sustainability Reporting of Finnish Defence Industry Companies The objective of this bachelors thesis was to provide more information and create better understanding about sustainability communication of Finnish defence industry companies and their use of UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). This thesis emphasized a communications point of view. The subject was approached using a combination of quantitative and qualitative methods. Quantitative material was collected from sustainability reports and sustainability webpages of companies which are members of Finnish Defence and Aerospace Industries PIA. This thesis concentrated on those companies
column2: which had selected SDGs close to them. A short questionnaire was also sent to these companies to create a versatile picture behind the statistics and to enlighten reasons behind choosing a certain SDG for the companys sustainability communication.

column1: The collected material was analysed using discourse analysis paying special attention to nine themes the companies in this study had brought up in their sustainability communication. The subject was also discussed from the perspective of company values
column2: how they reflect sustainability communication and the selection of the closest SDGs.

column1: Research results of this thesis indicate that companies in this study used SDGs in their sustainability communication in a flexible and practical manner. Sustainability measures were chosen to gain the biggest possible influence. This was also reflected in the selection of the SDGs. Consumption and production methods were seen as an important means to affect sustainability. The companies in this study also stressed the importance of innovation and co-operation. The defence industry companies in this study reacted to their limited possibilities to openly communicate about company actions by stressing their commitment to following rules and regulations as well as data security. Companies also elevated the theme of taking care of employees in their communication

column1: 193;"Precision time protocol on the Raspberry Pi single board computer This thesis project is a collaboration between the student and Savonia University of Applied Science to determine the feasibility of synchronising Raspberry Pi single-board computers through the utilisation of the Precision Time Protocol within a sub-microsecond offset between the devices.

A LAN network was assembled with multiple Raspberry Pi devices which were synchronised with the PTP protocol and experiments were run where they activated GPIO pins simultaneously. Those activations were
measured and the offsets were compared with a digital oscilloscope.
The synchronisation software was also deactivated and the devices were measured over the course of a few
days to determine if there is any drift in the offsets after synchronisation was deactivated.
The GPIO pin measurements in the synchronisation stage were tested at different incremental activation

states and though sub-microsecond synchronisation was never observed the devices did show synchronisation within ten microseconds. The network also showed a lot of jitter and a lack of synchronisation the more frequent the GPIO pin activations were made.
Secondarily when the synchronisation software was disabled the devices on the network showed significant
drift from each other.";0;NO_INDICATION_OF_COLLABORATION;Precision time protocol on the Raspberry Pi single board computer;Savonia UAS;Faherty

column1: 201;"Tekoðlyn hy—dyntðminen organisaation strategisen pððt—ksenteon ja kehitysty—n tukena Artificial Intelligence (AI) has emerged as a significant factor in organizations strategic decision-making and development efforts. This thesis examines how organizations utilize AI to support strategic decision-making in business and what impacts it has on leadership and organizational structures. The study investigates how AI can be leveraged within the organizations current technological framework and how it is expected to transform strategic work in the near future.
The research was conducted using a triangulation method. This method combines various research methods and theories
column2: including surveys and thematic interviews. The target group for this thesis consisted of Finnish organizations from various industries that are already utilizing AI in their strategic decision-making or are planning its wider use. The data was analyzed using qualitative content analysis methods. Survey was sent to 298 people
column3: of whom 48 responded. This represents 16 percent answer rate and is considered to give a reasonable basis for analysis.

column1: The results indicate that AI was widely used to improve operational efficiency and optimize processes. In strategic decision-making
column2: AI was particularly applied in predictive analytics
column3: scenario planning
column4: and risk management. The adoption of AI has made leadership more data-driven and analytical
column5: while also increasing the demands for technical expertise among leaders. The challenges identified included developing employees skills regarding using AI in their work
column6: integrating AI into existing systems
column7: and addressing ethical and data privacy concerns.

column1: Based on the research
column2: organizations should invest in employee training
column3: data quality
column4: and adherence to ethical principles when utilizing AI. AI is expected to significantly transform strategic work in the near future
column5: and its integration into business processes is seen as a key factor for maintaining competitiveness.";0;NO_INDICATION_OF_COLLABORATION;Tekoðlyn hy—dyntðminen organisaation strategisen pððt—ksenteon ja kehitysty—n tukena;Savonia UAS;Brunou

The study evaluates the extent and effectiveness of banking support mechanisms through both qualitative and quantitative lenses.

column1: 276;"Beyond price: Understanding quality investing : understanding private investor perception of quality investing

through semi-structured interviews Quality investing has gained popularity as an investment approach during recent years. Quality investing lacks a commonly agreed on definition and thus it could be of interest to understand how private investors perceive the metrics related to quality investing. Earlier research has established that profitability
column2: capital allocation and competitive advantages have a positive impact on the quality of a company. This thesis used semi-structured interviews as a research method to answer the following research questions 1) Does private investor perception of quality companies align with research on sustainable competitive advantages? 2) Does private investor perception of quality companies align with research on capital allocation and management? 3) Does private investor perception of quality companies align with research on return on capital?. Through semi-structured interviews
column3: it was possible to gain data on how private investors perceived the metrics that attributed to quality. The interview answers were analyzed through contextual analysis to gain a rich understanding of the answers. By comparing the answers to presented research on capital allocation
column4: return on capital and competitive advantage the research questions could be answered. The results indicated that private investors perception of quality metrics didnÇt align or align modestly with presented research. This indicates that private investors might not be aware of the metrics that contribute to a companyÇs quality. The results also implied that private investors had a varying definition for what quality is. Due to the limited size of the sample
column5: the results should not be generalized
column6: and further research is recommended.";0;NO_INDICATION_OF_COLLABORATION;"Beyond price: Understanding quality investing : understanding private investor perception of quality investing

column1: 330;"Network element credential verification in MantaRay NM Computer networks are a central part of critical infrastructure in modern society. At their current scale
column2: networks have become so complex and extensive that
column3: without standardized network management functions and systems
column4: maintaining their operation and reliability would be extremely challenging. This thesis explores network management (NM)
column5: with a particular focus on Nokia's Network management system (NMS)
column6: MantaRay NM. The goal of this work is to develop a test automation program for MantaRay NM
column7: which will verify the correctness of the credentials entered for network elements within the management system.

column1: The thesis is divided into two parts. The first part involves a literature review on network management and the MantaRay NM system. This review also includes an examination of MantaRay NM's internal application
column2: Network Element Access Control (NEAC)
column3: and the network protocols it uses
column4: which are essential for implementing the test automation program. The literature review revealed that network management is guided by standards such as ITU-T X.700: Management Framework for Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) for CCITT applications. This standard defines five key network management functions
column5: which can be implemented using various protocols
column6: such as the Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP). These network management functions are utilized in network management systems to monitor and maintain various network elements
column7: such as 4G and 5G base stations and the networks they form.

column1: The second part of the thesis focuses on the development of the test automation program using the Python programming language. The purpose of this program is to retrieve the credentials entered into NEAC for different protocols and test their functionality on the network elements. The program identifies which protocols have been assigned to a selected network element in NEAC and indicates which protocols passed the tests and which did not. This information is valuable for troubleshooting during the integration of network elements.
The result of this work is a functional test automation program that
column2: in addition to protocol-specific test functions
column3: includes features such as logging and error handling. The program effectively identifies potential error-prone points during troubleshooting. Test functions for five of the most used protocols have been implemented
column4: and in the future
column5: the testing capabilities could be expanded to cover a larger number of protocols
column6: further automating the troubleshooting process.";1;NOKIA_COLLABORATION;Network element credential verification in MantaRay NM;Tampere University;Laulainen

column1: 347;"WLAN-pohjaisten paikannusmenetelmien toteutus ja kehitys Wireless Local Area Network (WLAN) -based positioning has become a very promising wireless positioning method due to the widespread use of WLAN networks and the development of the technology. WLAN-based positioning is mostly used indoors in place of satellite positioning
column2: in places such as factories or hospitals. The purpose of this thesis is to examine how WLAN-based positioning can be implemented and to display some of the recent developments related to the technology.

column1: This thesis is a literature review and is divided into three parts. The first part of the thesis presents the theoretical background needed to understand the basics of wireless local area networks
column2: taking into account the details mostly related to positioning. The second part of this thesis lists and describes in detail the various implementation methods for WLAN-based positioning
column3: which can be divided into three categories. These categories are fingerprinting
column4: geometric methods
column5: and hybrid methods. The last part of this thesis takes a look at the technological developments affecting WLAN-based positioning from year 2018 to 2025. In this chapter
column6: a few examples of specific WLAN-based positioning solutions are also presented.

column1: The most important benefit of WLAN-based positioning is the ability to use it with existing infrastructure. The use of this technology often relies only on the pre-existing wireless local area networks
column2: which are widely used in all urban environments
column3: making the implementation notably less complex when compared to other wireless positioning methods. From the methods presented in this thesis
column4: fingerprinting is the most used due to its cost-effectiveness and ease of implementation. However
column5: various hybrid methods achieve more accurate measurements and are being developed extensively. Based on the literature
column6: the developments in new and existing technologies
column7: such as artificial intelligence and new standards
column8: are driving notable improvements in WLAN-based positioning. Automatisation of measurements
column9: built-in support for multi-device simultaneous use
column10: and greatly increased data rates are
column11: among others

column1: 350;"Generic Fronthaul Model Interface for SoC Simulators The development of System-on-Chips is a complex cycle
column2: where system modeling can be used to help verify the system's functionalities and identify flaws before fabrication. System-on-Chip models can be implemented with multiple modeling approaches
column3: ranging between functional
column4: reference
column5: and architectural models. Each have their advantages and may be used in tandem during development. One of the key challenges in system modeling is having a standardized method for interfacing with each model.

column1: To provide support for functional modeling
column2: this thesis presents a generic fronthaul model interface that was designed to solve an interoperability issue between fronthaul models. The interface makes it possible to communicate with the fronthaul model in a simulation environment. Furthermore
column3: the same interface can be used for sending configuration data to the fronthaul model.

column1: This research was conducted for Nokia Networks and utilizes their fronthaul subsystem model made in C++ in a simulated environment. The developed interface was used for interfacing between two fronthaul models. Also
column2: a dedicated configuration subsystem was made and tested for performing external register writes to the fronthaul model using the same interface. To validate the implementation
column3: two test cases were made: one to demonstrate the ability of one model instance to communicate with another one
column4: and one test to evaluate the implementation of the external data writing to the fronthaul model's registers.

column1: The structure of this thesis includes a literary review of the technologies used in fronthaul models
column2: related work
column3: and the components or blocks used in this thesis. After this
column4: the test environment
column5: test scenarios
column6: and their results are discussed. The thesis concludes with a discussion on future work based on the test results.

column1: This work lays groundwork for future interoperability solutions in SoC simulations. The results were positive and both test cases were proven successful. The implementation for the fronthaul model showed promising possibilities
column2: enabling interfacing between the fronthaul models
column3: while also enabling external access to the model's internal registers. The proposed work can be used as tools in the future to make simulations more akin to real life scenarios.

column1: This machine-learning problem is a supervised classification-learning problem. LTE Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) were used as training features
column2: where these KPIs are measured on a cell level every 15 minutes in a private LTE network deployed in an automated port terminal. Conversely
column3: the latency on a device level every 10 seconds was collected from the same network and utilized as the model label. The main challenge of the model training is the class imbalance of the label
column4: where only 0.14% of the data samples contain high-latency labels. Class balancing techniques were applied to the data such as down-sampling the majority class and over-sampling the minority class. After that
column5: linear
column6: tree-based
column7: and Support Vector Machines (SVM) training models were selected to train the data.

column1: The final outcome was achieving 0.83 recall and 0.5 precision in detecting high-latency incidents. Furthermore
column2: the features used by the tree-based algorithms during the training were analyzed. It was found that the cell-level average latency
column3: the Physical Resource Block (PRB) usage
column4: residual Block Error Rate (BLER)
column5: and re-transmission rate in the Radio Link Control (RLC) layer were the strongest features being used to detect high-latency short-time peaks on the device level.

column1: 352;"Time synchronized co-simulation for pre-silicon SoC validation As the complexity of SoC (System-on-Chip) increases
column2: there is a need for new novel design and testing techniques. One way the chip manufacturers aim to speed up the development process is by using comprehensive pre-silicon SoC validation. Pre-silicon SoC validation
column3: also known as validation left-shifting is a process in which virtual platforms are used to test the integration of hardware and software components before the physical chip has been manufactured.

column1: Virtual platforms enable the creation of co-simulation environments
column2: in which simulators and emulators are used to concurrently model software and hardware components. An important as pect of a co-simulation environment is its ability to provide time synchronization between components so that the timing correctness and timing functionalities can be verified.

column1: This thesis is written in commission to Nokia Solutions and Networks Oy
column2: and it proposes a time synchronized co-simulation environment for pre-silicon SoC validation. The environment is built around Nokias in house developed TSS (Target SoC Simulator). TSS is a simulator for SoC simulation
column3: verification
column4: and validation and it provides mechanisms for connecting different types of models at different abstraction levels. TSS uses transaction objects to model register accesses between models. The environment includes QEMU (Quick Emulator) for processor and software modeling and a commercial logic simulator for RTL (Register Transfer Level) hardware modeling.

column1: The environment utilizes internal time synchronization support of TSS. Instruction counting mechanism of QEMU is utilized to derive an estimate of elapsed time of machine instructions executed by a guest processor. QEMU models a guest processor by translating guest code to host code in so called translation blocks. After each translation block execution
column2: elapsed time is calculated and passed to commercial simulator to advance its time accordingly. This procedure creates time synchronization between software and hardware models. Inter process communication methods such as named pipe and named semaphore are used to send timing information from QEMU to simulator.

column1: The proposed co-simulation environment is evaluated in different co-simulation setups to obtain speed and accuracy metrics. It is observed that the accuracy of the co-simulation depends on the type of software that is executed on guest processor modelled by QEMU. This is due to the fact that translation blocks created by QEMU vary in length. According to measurements
column2: the blocks tend to get longer when guest processor executes computationally intensive software. Longer translation blocks reduce the accuracy of the co-simulation
column3: but it improves the speed because of less frequent need of communication between QEMU and simulator.

column1: The communication delay between QEMU and commercial simulator is observed to be a major bottleneck that reduces the co-simulation performance. The large communication overhead stems from the usage of time consuming inter process communication methods. Another performance limiting factor is non-optimized transaction handling by RTL model
column2: that continuously polls for upcoming transactions. The future work regarding the proposed co-simulation environment should focus on improving the communication infrastructure between QEMU and simulator and optimizing transaction handling on the RTL side.";1;NOKIA_COLLABORATION;Time synchronized co-simulation for pre-silicon SoC validation;Tampere University;Rintakangas

column1: 353;"Concurrent SA and NSA mode operation of NR cell in 5G Cloud RAN The evolution of 5G mobile communication systems has transformed modern communication by enabling high speed data rates
column2: short latency
column3: and the ability to connect a large number of devices. To meet these growing demands efficiently
column4: the network architecture is shifting from traditional hardware-based RAN toward Cloud-based Radio Access Network (C-RAN)
column5: which offers flexibility
column6: scalability
column7: and centralized resource management.

column1: 5G networks operates in NSA and SA deployment modes. NSA uses the existing LTE core to speed up early rollouts
column2: while SA relies fully on the 5G Core to deliver advanced features. However
column3: current gNB implementations typically support only one mode at a time
column4: making it difficult for operators to migrate smoothly to SA. The transition from NSA to SA is complex due to infrastructure costs
column5: device compatibility
column6: and the need to maintain service continuity for legacy NSA users. To overcome this limitation
column7: a deployment option known as concurrent SA and NSA mode allows both modes to operate within the same 5G NR cell and cloud gNB. This mode combines so called NSA option 3x and SA option 2 architectures together. The concurrent mode allows operators to maintain NSA services while gradually deploying SA capabilities
column8: thereby ensuring a smooth migration path toward full 5G SA. Moreover
column9: it enhances network resilience through independent operation of the two modes
column11: and reduces operational costs by minimizing the need for separate infrastructure.

column1: This thesis work
column2: carried out at Nokias Espoo laboratory within the MN Testing and Verification team
column3: focuses on implementing and validating concurrent SA/NSA operation in a 5G Cloud RAN environment. The considered test setup was tested through cell availability verification
column4: concurrent SA and NSA user connection via admission control
column5: L3 signalling validation
column6: and end-user downlink throughput measurement in the cloud gNB. The results are analysed to see that modes function reliably in the same cell without degradation
column7: demonstrating a practical path toward flexible
column8: cost-efficient 5G evolution in cloud-based networks.";1;NOKIA_COLLABORATION;Concurrent SA and NSA mode operation of NR cell in 5G Cloud RAN;Tampere University;Khan

column1: 354;"RTL Development Optimization by Reusing SystemC Model Testbench Modern systems are growing increasingly complex
column2: demanding equally sophisticated Systemon-Chip (SoC) solutions that require extensive design and verification effort. Verification alone accounts for the majority of the total development time and cost
column3: largely due to the slow and resource-intensive nature of Hardware Description Language (HDL) based simulation. To address this challenge
column4: this thesis explores a shift-left verification methodology aimed at enabling earlier validation and faster tape-out.

column1: The proposed approach reuses existing SystemC transaction-level models (TLMs) to verify corresponding Register Transfer Level (RTL) implementations through a SystemVerilog Direct Programming Interface (DPI) interface. Unlike traditional co-simulation frameworks
column2: the SystemC model and RTL remain decoupled
column3: communicating only through shared memory provided by the operating system. This setup allows the high-level SystemC tests to execute independently and efficiently while the Unified Verification Methodology (UVM) environment acts solely as a passthrough interface
column4: minimizing overhead.

column1: By leveraging the speed and abstraction of SystemC
column2: this methodology reduces dependency on licensed simulators
column3: decreases engineering effort
column4: and accelerates functional validation. This thesis aims to quantify the achievable verification coverage
column5: measure reductions in verification effort
column6: and assess the mutual consistency between SystemC models and RTL designs. The results demonstrate that SystemC-driven verification can achieve coverage comparable to traditional UVM for the evaluated tests
column7: while reducing simulation cost and engineering workload. However
column8: UVM remains necessary for timing-critical and corner-case verification
column9: positioning the proposed flow as a complementary acceleration mechanism rather than a replacement. Through this
column10: the proposed solution seeks to demonstrate a scalable and cost-effective verification framework for future SoC development.

column1: For the evaluated test suite
column2: the proposed SystemC-driven verification flow achieved functional coverage nearly identical to the UVM baseline
column3: demonstrating that the decoupled co-simulation does not compromise verification quality. In addition
column4: the methodology delivered a substantial performance improvement
column5: reducing verification runtime and memory consumption by approximately 50%. These results highlight the practicality and efficiency of the approach
column6: reinforcing its potential as a scalable acceleration mechanism for SoC verification.";0;NO_INDICATION_OF_COLLABORATION;RTL Development Optimization by Reusing SystemC Model Testbench;Tampere University;Salman

column1: 358;"Synchronization of SoC models using PTP for improved validation Post-silicon verification or validation is a key step during development of a SoC (System on Chip). Its goal is to find bugs that might get unnoticed during verification and to ensure that non-functional features of the final chip work correctly. However
column2: finding hardware errors only during validation stage can be costly due to possible additional tape-outs and development costs. To tackle this problem pre-silicon validation can be utilized. It uses emulated and simulated environments RTL-models (Register Transfer Level)
column3: and testing software created for validation to be utilized even before tape-out. In addition to earlier design error discovery
column4: it also reduces time needed for traditional validation to only non-functional testing increasing time-to-market and reducing overall cost of development.

column1: While verification focuses on testing the functionality of singular SoC
column2: there might be final use cases where several SoCs need to cooperate. One of these scenarios is 5G RAN (Radio Access Network)
column3: where the system can consist of multiple separate devices with own SoCs. To ensure correct functionality of 5G RAN system
column4: synchronization between its parts of it needs to be established. This can be achieved by utilizing one of the most widely used synchronization protocols called PTP (Precision time protocol).

column1: This thesis is done as a commission to Nokia Solutions and networks and its goal is to add PTP synchronization capability to a QEMU based TSS (Target SoC Simulator) and synchronize two PHC (PTP Hardware Clock) models using it. To achieve this
column2: two QEMU-TSS instances were connected to each other using Unix socket based virtual ethernet and then operated using open-source application called ptp4l. Both instances use modified virtio-net module to enable timestamping of network packets as well as custom created PHC driver that manages the PHC-models. Performance of the final system is evaluated and compared to a system which utilizes software-based timestamping as well as to system where TSS-QEMU instances run freely.

column1: Results of this thesis show that while synchronization on model level can be achieved
column2: due to implementation of time propagation in QEMU
column3: the synchronization of entire system cannot be achieved with the current state of the TSS. This thesis however provides proof-of-concept implementation of PTP synchronization stack that keeps two models synchronized to a reasonable level. Potential future improvements could be addition of external synchronization mechanism that brings severely unsynchronized QEMUs closer to each other so that finer synchronization using PTP could be used. Another improvement is to connect PHC directly to the QEMU time to reduce propagation delay happening in the current implementation.";1;NOKIA_COLLABORATION;Synchronization of SoC models using PTP for improved validation;Tampere University;Trofimov

column1: 366;"Network Energy Saving in 5G-Advanced and Beyond Energy consumption in 5G networks has emerged as a critical concern
column2: with Base Stations (BS) accounting for a substantial portion of overall network power usage. While various energy-saving techniques have been proposed and studied
column3: cell Discontinuous Transmission and Reception (DTX/DRX) optimization remains relatively under-explored despite its potential for significant Network Energy Saving (NES). This thesis addresses this gap by developing a Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) framework to dynamically optimize DTX/DRX ON duration across multiple cells.

column1: A comprehensive System Level Simulator (SLS) was utilized and enhanced with multi-cell capabilities
column2: interference modelling and a dynamic power consumption model. The DRL framework employs a single-agent approach with centralized coordination
column3: enabling comprehensive network visibility and coordinated decisionmaking across cells. The reward function was carefully formulated to balance NES with Quality of Service (QoS) requirements.

column1: Critical insights emerged during agent training
column2: including reward misalignment issues where the agent optimized reward values rather than actual network performance. These challenges were addressed through reward function refinements and hyperparameter tuning. The results demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves considerable energy savings while maintaining QoS through dynamic DTX/DRX parameter optimization. Across diverse deployment configurations ranging from high-traffc to low-traffc scenarios
column3: static power consumption was reduced by more than 50% compared to the Always ON baseline
column4: whilst mean throughput degradation remained below 10%. Furthermore
column5: delay characteristics remained acceptable across all configurations
column6: confirming the viability of the DRL framework.";0;NO_INDICATION_OF_COLLABORATION;Network Energy Saving in 5G-Advanced and Beyond;Tampere University;Jayaweera

column1: 370;Managing company reputation during layoffs The role of reputation in a company's everyday business has only been recognized in the literature in recent decades. Reputation influences how willing stakeholders are to cooperate with the company
column2: and how they react to its actions. Achieving and maintaining desired reputation is based on the companys ability to fulfil stakeholder expectations. These expectations are created by the companys brand
column3: the values and norms of its stakeholders
column4: and external laws and regulations. Layoffs pose a threat to company reputation
column5: as the announcement alone can disappoint stakeholders. Previous literature has identified meeting stakeholder expectations as the foundation of company reputation
column6: which becomes particularly challenging during the layoffs process. The goal of this thesis was to identify different approaches companies can use to manage their reputation in layoff situations. To explore this
column7: a review of reputation and brand literature was utilized. The main finding was the identification of three approaches to reputation management: (1) the company strives to support its laid-off and remaining employees
column8: (2) the company strives to support its laid-off employees and (3) the company does not strive to support its employees. The conclusions made were observed empirically through multiple case study with three layoff cases. Empirical analysis validated the division made and supported existing theory but also questioned prior knowledge on reputation formation and management. The findings and conclusions attained in this thesis provide additional information and perspectives to reputation management in layoff situations. These findings can be useful for theory building and corporate decision-making. The phenomenon was examined from a high technology perspective and through a small sample
column9: so the results are not broadly generalizable.;0;NO_INDICATION_OF_COLLABORATION;Managing company reputation during layoffs;Oulu University;Posti

column1: 379;Building and automating a 5G radio unit software benchmarking environment This thesis focused on creating a new 5G radio unit (RU) software benchmarking environment for Nokias OUL2 department. The purpose of this study was to create a software benchmarking environment that could provide more realistic performance measurements than existing system component testing (SCT) environments. The new software benchmarking environment was needed for getting more realistic performance measurements for Layer 1 (L1)/L1Low RU software at early product lifecycle stages. Testing software at an earlier stage enabled faster feedback to the development teams. The faster feedback helped developers create fixes and new features faster. The SCT environments had some downsides. They could not test full radio software
column2: since the testing was done with isolated L1Low software component. Additionally
column3: the SCT environments could not test larger test configurations with multiple control plane (C-plane) real-time control (RTC) messages. This limited possible testing scenarios
column4: as the larger tests were needed to test worse case scenarios. The development of software benchmarking environment artifact was done using design science research methodology (DSRM). DSRM was selected as the research method as it supported creation of new artifacts. The existing SCT environments were used as a base when building the software benchmarking environment. The artifact had four iterations. Each of the iterations build on the last iteration. The artifact was evaluated by comparing it to an equivalent SCT environment. The new software benchmarking environment used full radio software and a baseband unit (BBU) emulator for testing. Additionally
column5: user plane (U-plane) in-phase and quadrature (IQ) data was included with C-plane RTC messages in created packet capture (PCAP) files. The PCAP files were used as test content for benchmarking tests. Use of full radio software
column6: BBU emulator
column7: and inclusion of IQ data helped make the software benchmarking environment more like real-world RU environments. The evaluation proved that the software benchmarking environment produced more realistic performance results compared to the equivalent SCT environment. The SCT environments used core calculation to estimate performance of larger configurations. Use of the new slot-based performance calculation allowed testing with multiple RTC messages. This made it possible to calculate actual performance for larger test configurations. In conclusion
column8: the created software benchmarking environment enabled more realistic RU software performance measurement.;0;NO_INDICATION_OF_COLLABORATION;Building and automating a 5G radio unit software benchmarking environment;Oulu University;Paldanius

column2: thereby serving as the commissioning organization for the project's overall scope. The project team then specified the purpose to create and assess a concept for an AI-powered Correlation Agent capable of identifying root causes of technical debt and supporting proactive maintenance.

column1: The development task was to conceptualize and prototype an AI agent that integrates structural code data with dispersed textual contexts such as Git commit messages and documentation
column2: in order to diagnose and mitigate technical debt. Nokia benefits from reduced maintenance costs
column3: faster workflows
column4: and improved code quality based on the potential of this solution.

column1: The study builds on theories of technical debt management
column2: agentic AI versus generative AI capabilities
column3: and correlation analysis as a statistical method for identifying relationships between key indicators and maintenance goals. The research employed a Design Sprint approach complemented by brainstorming sessions
column4: current-state mapping
column5: benchmarking of existing tools (CodeScene
column6: SonarQube
column7: Greptile
column9: and structured questionnaire interviews with IT Systems Specialists.

column1: The tools that are already available do not combine code analysis with contextual information
column2: which makes it more difficult to solve problems. Agentic AI
column3: due to its inherent multi-step reasoning capabilities
column4: is highly suitable for multi-step maintenance workflows and proactive risk detection. A prototype named Code Colleague (CoCo) demonstrated that a conversational interface enhances collaboration between developers and the AI agent.

column1: The Correlation AI Agent can significantly reduce manual investigation time
column2: accelerate fault correction
column3: and lower long-term maintenance costs. The focus of future development should be on integrating the Correlation AI Agent into a multi-agent chain
column4: defining inter-agent collaboration protocols
column5: and establishing auditing mechanisms for AI-driven processes.

column1: 147;"Smart Chatbot for Discovering Finnish Theses: A Conversational System Using RAG and Theseus Data This thesis presents a conversational chatbot system for discovering Finnish university theses. It is using ?Retrieval-Augmented Generation? (RAG) technology. The project was developed during an internship at Nokia Garage Espoo. It integrated into Nokia Stand
column2: a collaborative platform showcasing Nokia and Metropolia University partnerships.

column1: The traditional keyword-based search on Theseus.fi
column2: Finland's national thesis repository
column3: requires exact questions and multiple attempts to find relevant content. This thesis addresses these limitations by implementing a RAG-based chatbot. The chatbot understands semantic meaning and enables natural language interaction
column4: interpreting what users want rather than simply matching keywords.

column1: The system has two main parts. The first part is a mobile app built with React Native. The second part is a backend built with Node.js that uses RAG technology. The backend collects thesis information from Theseus.fi. It then turns this data into vectors using a model called all-MiniLM-L6-v2. The system stores 45 Nokia-related theses in its memory. When users ask questions
column2: the system finds the right theses and creates answers using ready-made templates. This makes the system very fast. It gives answers in less than 0.5 seconds. All development
column3: implementation
column4: and testing were conducted in a local development environment. Testing showed that semantic search successfully retrieves relevant theses regardless of terminology differences
column5: demonstrating that RAG technology improves academic search experiences compared to traditional keyword matching.";1;NOKIA_COLLABORATION;;;;;;

column1: 149;"Analysis and Visualization of Uplink Interference in LTE and 5G Networks Using RIP Counters This thesis presents the design and development of a Python based application utilizing Received Interference Power counters to analyze and visualize uplink interference in LTE and 5G networks
column2: commissioned by Nokia Solutions and Networks Oy. The program overcomes the issues of existing internal tools by allowing a fullspectrum analysis of outside of the time domain view. The program takes Received Interference Power (RIP) counter information from Base Transceiver Stations snapshots
column3: displays it in an interactive graphical user interface made with PyQT5 and Matplotlib
column4: and engineers using this program identify the nature and shape of the uplink interference which can assist in detecting the presence of Passive Intermodulation (PIM) in a time efficient manner.

column1: The foundations of uplink interference is based on the radio frequency theory and network architecture
column2: specifically the function of Physical Resource Blocks (PRBs) and propagation properties of wireless signals. The fundamental units of resource allocation in LTE and 5G are PRBs
column3: and RIP counters make it possible to measure the interference across these units. The power levels across these blocks are impacted by interference particularly PIM sources
column4: which show up in RIP plots as slopes. This visualization of the slope will make this tool greatly increase an engineer?s effectiveness in detecting and diagnosing network problems.";1;NOKIA_COLLABORATION;;;;;;

column1: 264;"React-Based Chatbot UI for Generative AI: a reusable and extensible framework for Nokia R&D The explosive development of Artificial Intelligence (AI) has revolutionized human-computer interaction
column2: with intelligent chatbots emerging as a pivotal technology in the digital landscape. In this thesis

column4: particularly by addressing internal operational requirements and private data protection.

column1: The primary objective is to create a chatbot that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to give users easy access to Nokia's internal knowledge base in addition to replicating conversational Artificial intelligence capabilities. The framework emphasizes scalability
column2: simplicity of customization
column3: and successful interaction with current Nokia platforms by leveraging React
column4: NodeJS
column5: Express and Docker technologies. This work sets the groundwork for future artificial intelligence-driven products at Nokia by advancing the theoretical understanding of chatbot generation and practical applications in artificial intelligence systems.

column1: 368;"Machine learning-based prediction of RSRP in cellular networks Proactive mobility management represents an important driver of reliable connectivity within fifth generation (5G)
column2: beyond-5G (B5G)
column3: and future sixth generation (6G) networks. In these contexts
column4: dense networks create difficulties for traditional reactive handover (HO) solutions. These HO decisions are largely dependent on reference signal received power (RSRP)
column5: and therefore precisely predicting future RSRP measurements can support more intelligent HOs. Industrial stakeholders
column6: such as Nokia
column7: are at the forefront of research efforts related to standardization within the 3rd Generation Partnership Project (3GPP) environment. In addition
column8: they are investigating artificial intelligence and machine learning (AI/ML) techniques to enhance radio resource management (RRM).

column1: This thesis presents an industry-driven case study done in collaboration with Nokia
column2: which utilizes real-world drive-test measurement data.  The thesis evaluates the feasibility of low-complexity ML models to predict RSRP by utilizing only the raw time series measurements and without relying on any auxiliary data. The models tested are linear regression (LR)
column3: random forest (RF)
column4: and k-nearest neighbors (KNN)
column5: which are evaluated under multiple realistic train-test strategies and are compared against the sample-and-hold (SnH) baseline. This approach reflects the practical constraints that are often present in real networks.

column1: The results indicate that simple ML models generally struggle to outperform the SnH baseline in most generalized prediction tasks. However
column2: in some more restricted scenarios
column3: e.g.
column4: UE-specific and site-specific prediction
column5: the RF model achieves modest and yet consistent improvements when compared to SnH. These results suggest that even though low-complexity ML models might be limited in their capacity to generalize
column6: they can provide appreciable gains in UE and site-specific environments. These findings are also largely consistent with industry views and support current 3GPP efforts in optimizing prediction accuracy and feasibility in proactive mobility management solutions by companies like Nokia.";1;NOKIA_COLLABORATION;;;;;;

column1: 394;"Something that connects people: Pluralising the Nokia design archive through social remembering Archives are documentary by-products generated in activities of both individuals and collectives. They are crucial actors in safeguarding individual and social memory and promoting democracy. Thus
column2: accessibility is an integral part of archival preservation. Since the turn of the millennium
column3: digitisation has been considered a transformative tool for both preservation and access.

column1: However
column2: accessibility is not a natural outcome of digitality. For one thing
column3: digital obsolescence affects the stability of records. For another
column4: the design of digital interfaces often disguises the assumptions and biases of the process. Furthermore
column5: existing archival user research has predominantly focused on academic users and their archival research experiences
column6: which limits the possibility of reading wider archival users.

column1: Combining Stuart Hall?s notion of ?living archive? and the Records Continuum Model
column2: this thesis recognises the plurality in interpreting archival materials and the possibility of using them outside academic settings. I conducted a single-case study on the Nokia Design Archive
column3: exploring the potential for the archive to engage the public through social remembering.

column1: For this thesis
column2: I introduced the concept of ?social rememberer?
column3: referring to the group of users who are neither experienced archival researchers nor the creators of the records (i.e.
column4: Nokia designers)
column5: yet are curious about the archive because of their previous experiences with Nokia?s design products. I studied six social rememberers? explorations with the archive through semi-structured interviews. In addition
column6: I designed a set of interview probes
column7: A Souvenir from Your Memory
column8: to elicit their recollections and prompt the discussion about archival pluralisation.

column1: The reflexive thematic analysis reveals three main themes: (1) dimensions of remembering
column2: (2) navigating between memory and archival logic
column3: (3) embedding the archive into its social life. These findings together addressed the research questions of this thesis. Firstly
column4: rememberers do not only rely on their memories and experiences with Nokia?s design when exploring the archive. Their present interest and expertise eventually enriched their journey in the archive. Secondly
column5: the major challenges for rememberers are the mismatch between archival vocabulary and their everyday language
column6: the technical limitations of the current digital portal
column7: and the communication of archival boundaries. Four design opportunities were proposed during the discussion: (1) creating visual pathways
column9: (3) presenting a curated storyline
column10: and (4) enabling natural language interactions. However
column11: these proposals should be further evaluated before implementation. Lastly
column12: these findings together present the possibility of linking this archive with other collections and knowledge sources
column13: which implies the importance of interoperability in archival pluralisation.

column1: This thesis contributes to the field of digital cultural heritage and archival user study
column2: focusing on the dynamics between social remembering and archival interfaces. In addition
column3: my design for the interview probe
column4: A Souvenir from Your Memory
column5: explored interdisciplinary and participatory techniques in archival user research. The format and learnings can be utilised by other practitioners and researchers in the field of digital cultural heritage
column6: open knowledge
column7: and possibly material culture.";0;NO_INDICATION_OF_COLLABORATION;;;;;;

column1: 395;"Implementation and evaluation of 5G network slicing and URSP in a physical test environment As mobile technologies advance and the number of connected devices increases
column2: communication networks must continuously evolve. To meet the needs of mobile technologies 5G network introduces a new enhanced core and RAN architecture. A key innovation this new architecture enables is network slicing
column3: which allows the network to be divided into virtual slices
column4: each optimized for specific use cases such as autonomous driving
column5: UL or DL prioritization
column6: ultra-low latency applications
column7: or security-prioritized communications.

column1: Another key innovation is the User Equipment Route Selection Policy (URSP)
column2: which enables users to subscribe to specific network slices based on their needs
column3: providing enhanced connectivity for applications such as secure work profiles or high-speed gaming. These new 5G technologies improve the overall data speed and delay and provide new use cases in automation and factories with ultra-reliability and consistency in the network.

column1: This thesis plans and implements a new gNB configuration file to Nokia's physical 5G test environment. The reason for creating a new gNB configuration file is to implement network slicing and standardize the configuration file architecture among the Nokia FiVe test network. The new configuration is tested with a few special slices
column2: including URSP technology and Nokia 5G 360 camera use cases
column3: where the benefits of sliced network architecture are shown.

column1: In both measurement cases with special slices
column2: we compared the device capabilities in the network between the default slice and the special slice dedicated to the device data traffic. In the measurements
column3: we see a clear difference between the default slice and low latency slice performance when using the Nokia 5G 360 camera as a testing device. With the URSP test
column4: we can see a clear difference in DL throughput values between two applications in the same UE. The application dedicated to a special slice got a consistent network connection
column5: while the application in the default slice got a lot of variance in the throughput.";1;NOKIA_COLLABORATION;;;;;;

column1: 396;"Predicting trajectories of peripheral agents for safe mobile robot navigation in industrial environments This thesis investigates short-horizon trajectory prediction of surrounding agents (humans and robots) as a proposed enabler for safer
column2: more efficient control of Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs) in industrial environments. Conducted at Nokia Bell Labs
column3: the work leverages the company?s Autonomous Robot Orchestration Solution
column4: which provides factory-wide situational awareness
column5: enabling trajectory predictions to be fed into the controller for collision anticipation.

column1: A reproducible pipeline is developed and evaluated to produce explainable predictions for the controller
column2: suitable for future certification. The pipeline includes video-based detection and homography transformation
column3: data filtering
column4: deterministic predictors (Constant Velocity; Constant Turn-Rate and Velocity)
column5: and a proposed hybrid physics-aware method under development that seeks to balance interpretability and adaptability.

column1: Models are assessed on public pedestrian datasets and on in-house recordings of human and robot motion using Average Displacement Error (ADE) and Final Displacement Error (FDE) metrics. Results are reported as distributions; the acceptable accuracy range ultimately depends on safety margins to be specified in relevant safety standards and controller requirements. Within this evaluation
column2: deterministic baselines provide strong short-horizon reference points. The proposed hybrid method has been iteratively improved and currently shows advantages over the baselines on the robot-motion dataset
column3: whereas performance on pedestrian datasets and particularly on long-horizon FDE remains challenging.

column1: Overall
column2: the pipeline is intended for integration with Nokia Bell Labs? orchestrator to enable anticipatory safety in multi-agent
column3: dynamic industrial environments.";1;NOKIA_COLLABORATION;;;;;;

column1: 450;"Network element credential verification in MantaRay NM Computer networks are a central part of critical infrastructure in modern society. At their current scale
column2: networks have become so complex and extensive that
column3: without standardized network management functions and systems
column4: maintaining their operation and reliability would be extremely challenging. This thesis explores network management (NM)
column5: with a particular focus on Nokia's Network management system (NMS)
column6: MantaRay NM. The goal of this work is to develop a test automation program for MantaRay NM
column7: which will verify the correctness of the credentials entered for network elements within the management system.

column1: The thesis is divided into two parts. The first part involves a literature review on network management and the MantaRay NM system. This review also includes an examination of MantaRay NM's internal application
column2: Network Element Access Control (NEAC)
column3: and the network protocols it uses
column4: which are essential for implementing the test automation program. The literature review revealed that network management is guided by standards such as ITU-T X.700: Management Framework for Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) for CCITT applications.
